statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input consists of a single integer n, which is the number of rows and columns in the maze. The following n lines describe the maze. Each line starts and ends with a 0, and contains only 0s and 1s. The value 0 represents an empty space and the value 1 represents a wall.
statement.outputFormat: Print the path traversal in a clockwise direction, if it is possible to traverse the maze. If it is not possible, print -1.
statement.notes: The path traversal follows these rules: always move clockwise, if a wall is encountered, backtrack, otherwise, add the current cell to the path

examples.explanation: The path traversal is 0001001 in a clockwise direction.

hints.text: Start by finding the first empty cell in the maze.

editorial.title: Maze Traversal Bug Fix Editorial
editorial.intuition: A naive approach would be to traverse the maze using a breadth-first search algorithm. However, this is not efficient and may not work for large mazes. We can solve this problem using backtracking, by trying all possible paths from each cell and backtracking when we reach a wall or if we reach a cell with no valid path.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force solution would be to try all possible paths from each cell and backtracking when we reach a wall or if we reach a cell with no valid path. However, this solution has a high time complexity of O(2^n), hence, not efficient for large mazes.
editorial.betterSolution: We can solve this problem using backtracking, by trying all possible paths from each cell and backtracking when we reach a wall or if we reach a cell with no valid path. This solution has a time complexity of O(n^2), making it efficient for large mazes.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use backtracking to try all possible paths from each cell. Start by finding the first empty cell in the maze. Then, for each cell, try all possible paths and backtrack when we reach a wall or if we reach a cell with no valid path.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is based on the fact that we are using backtracking to try all possible paths from each cell. This ensures that we are exploring all possible solutions and finding the correct one.
editorial.dryRun: To dry run the solution, start by finding the first empty cell in the maze. Then, for each cell, try all possible paths and backtrack when we reach a wall or if we reach a cell with no valid path.
commonMistakes: Not backtracking when reaching a wall or a cell with no valid path
